researchNotes: Expanded 2026-07 pass: drained the two Puzzles-tagged entries that were sitting unused in aptitude-evidence-stash.md since the original build (Q101 factory-necessity, Q102 digit-counting), added a real 5-person blood-relation puzzle found via fresh web research, and rounded out the archetype coverage (circular seating, position-from-ends ranking) since TNPSC puzzles repeatedly draw from a small set of well-known shapes. Every question now carries a 'trick' — a reusable fast-solving method — since Puzzles is exactly the skill where a memorized technique saves the most time under exam pressure.

reading.intro: Puzzles is 1 of 6 named Reasoning skills. The genre overlaps with Logical Reasoning, so this topic focuses on archetypes NOT already covered there: comparison chains, age puzzles, family-relation/blood-relation puzzles, linear and circular seating arrangements, distribution puzzles, and position-counting puzzles — all extremely common TNPSC puzzle shapes. Each entry below includes the worked method AND a named trick so you can recognize and solve the pattern fast on exam day.

qa.question: Method — comparison chain (not all four compared directly, must chain through clues): D is taller than C but not as tall as B. C is taller than A. Who is the tallest?
qa.answer: Chain it: B > D > C > A. So B is the tallest. The key technique: write each clue as an inequality, then merge them into one ordered chain rather than trying to compare all pairs mentally.
qa.tip: Write every comparison as a '>' sign the moment you read it, then physically merge the inequality chains on paper. Never try to hold more than 2 comparisons in your head at once.

qa.question: Method — classic age puzzle: A father is 4 times as old as his son. In 20 years, he will be twice as old as his son. Find their current ages.
qa.answer: Let son's current age = x, father = 4x. In 20 years: father = 4x+20, son = x+20. Condition: 4x+20 = 2(x+20) → 4x+20 = 2x+40 → 2x=20 → x=10. Son is 10, father is 40.
qa.tip: Always assign the variable to whichever age is expressed as a multiple of the other (son=x, father=kx), then add the SAME future-year offset to both ages before applying the new ratio — never apply the new ratio to today's ages by mistake.

qa.question: Method — family-relation puzzle: Pointing to a photograph, a man says, 'She is the daughter of my mother's only son.' How is the woman in the photo related to the man?
qa.answer: His daughter. 'My mother's only son' = the man himself (since he's referring to his own mother, and he's an only son). So 'her father' = the man, making the woman his daughter.
qa.tip: Whenever a puzzle says 'my mother's/father's [relation]', immediately substitute the actual person before reading further — 'my mother's only son' almost always resolves to the speaker himself (if male) or his only brother (if the speaker is female with a brother). Resolve the substitution first, then answer.

qa.question: Method — linear seating arrangement: Five friends P, Q, R, S, T sit in a row. Q is immediately to the right of P. R is at one end. S is between Q and T. T is not adjacent to P. Find the order.
qa.answer: Work from the most fixed clue first: R is at an end — try R at the left end. P-Q must be adjacent with Q right of P, and S is between Q and T, with T not adjacent to P. Testing R,P,Q,S,T: P-Q adjacent ✓, S between Q&T ✓, T not adjacent to P (T is at position 5, P at position 2, not adjacent) ✓, R at an end ✓. Order: R, P, Q, S, T.
qa.tip: Place the clue that fixes an absolute position (an 'end' or 'exact seat number') first, then apply strict adjacency clues (immediately left/right of), and use negative clues ('not adjacent to') only at the end to eliminate remaining candidates — negative clues are the weakest starting point because they rule out very little on their own.

qa.question: Method — distribution/sharing puzzle: A sum of money is divided among A, B, and C so that A gets twice as much as B, and B gets thrice as much as C. If C gets ₹600, find the total sum.
qa.tip: Chain the ratios into one combined ratio first: A:B:C = 6:3:1 (since A=2B=2×3C=6C). Then 'C's share = 1 part = ₹600', so total = (6+3+1) parts = 10×600 = ₹6,000 — faster than solving share-by-share.

qa.question: Real exam (Part B Reasoning, 2025_q page 97 Q101): 'A factory always has ___.' Choose from Electricity / Chimney / Workers / Files — which one MUST every factory have?
qa.answer: Workers. A factory can technically run without paid electricity (older/manual setups), without a chimney (many modern factories have none), and without paper files — but it cannot be a factory, by definition, without workers/production activity taking place.
qa.tip: For 'X always has ___' logical-necessity questions, don't pick the option that's merely common or typical — ask 'could X exist without this feature?' Eliminate anything you can imagine a real-world exception for, and keep only the option that's true by the very definition of X.

qa.question: Real exam (Part B Reasoning, 2025_q page 97 Q102): A book has 366 pages, numbered from 1. How many total digits are used to number all the pages?
qa.answer: Split by digit-length band: pages 1–9 → 9 pages × 1 digit = 9 digits. Pages 10–99 → 90 pages × 2 digits = 180 digits. Pages 100–366 → 267 pages × 3 digits = 801 digits. Total = 9 + 180 + 801 = 990 digits.
qa.tip: Formula for any N-page book (N≥100): digits = 9 (for pages 1–9) + 180 (for pages 10–99) + (N−99)×3 (for pages 100 to N). Never count digit-by-digit — split into 1-digit/2-digit/3-digit page bands and multiply.

qa.question: Real blood-relation puzzle (web-research): A, B, C, D and E are family members — there are two fathers, two sons, two wives, three males and two females. The teacher is the wife of a lawyer, who is the son of a doctor. E is not male and not the wife of a professional. C is the youngest, D is the eldest, B is male. How is D related to E?
qa.answer: D is E's husband. Working the tree: D (eldest) is the doctor; his son is the lawyer; the lawyer's wife is the teacher. With B male and E female (and E not a professional's wife, so E is likely the doctor's own wife), D and E form the eldest married pair — D is E's husband.
qa.tip: For multi-clue family puzzles, draw a tree top-to-bottom by generation as you read each clue, labeling M/F next to each name — resolve 'occupation' clues (doctor/lawyer/teacher) into the tree slots before answering the relationship question, since the profession chain is usually the key that unlocks who's who.

qa.question: Same puzzle as above (web-research): Who are the two females in the group A, B, C, D, E?
qa.answer: E and A. B is stated male; D (eldest, the doctor) pairs as husband to E; the lawyer (D's son) is male, leaving A and C as the remaining slots — cross-checking against 'two wives, three males, two females' places A as the lawyer's wife (the teacher) and confirms E and A as the two females.
qa.tip: Once you've built the family tree for one question in a linked puzzle set, reuse it directly for follow-up questions on the same puzzle rather than re-deriving from scratch — TNPSC often asks 2-3 questions off a single puzzle passage.

qa.question: Method — circular seating arrangement: Six friends A, B, C, D, E, F sit around a circular table, all facing the center. B is second to the right of A. C is immediately left of B. D is directly opposite A. E is immediately right of D. Where does F sit relative to A?
qa.answer: Fix A's seat as the reference point, numbered position 0, with seats 0-5 running clockwise ('to the right'). B = second to the right of A = position 2. C = immediately left of B (one step anticlockwise) = position 1. D = opposite A = position 3. E = immediately right of D = position 4. Only position 5 remains for F — which is one step anticlockwise from A, i.e., immediately to A's left.
qa.tip: Fix one person's seat as position 0 before placing anyone else, then convert every 'right'/'left'/'opposite' clue into a fixed clockwise seat number and draw the circle — clockwise and anticlockwise are meaningless without a fixed starting reference.

qa.question: Method — position-from-both-ends puzzle: In a row of children, Ravi is 7th from the left end and 12th from the right end. How many children are in the row?
qa.answer: Total = (position from left) + (position from right) − 1 = 7 + 12 − 1 = 18 children.
qa.tip: Formula: Total in a row = (position from one end) + (position from the other end) − 1. The '−1' avoids double-counting the person himself, who is counted in both positions. This instantly solves any such question without listing every seat.

qa.question: Method — two-generation age-sum puzzle: The sum of the ages of a father and son is 60 years. Six years ago, the father's age was 5 times the son's age. Find their present ages.
qa.answer: Let son's present age = x, father's = 60−x. Six years ago: father = 60−x−6 = 54−x, son = x−6. Condition: 54−x = 5(x−6) → 54−x = 5x−30 → 84 = 6x → x = 14. Son is 14, father is 46.
qa.tip: When a puzzle gives a SUM of two ages (not a multiple), let one variable be x and derive the other as (sum − x) — don't introduce a second unknown. Then apply the past/future offset to both expressions before using the given ratio, exactly as in single-generation age puzzles.

questions.prompt: P is shorter than Q but taller than R. Q is shorter than S. Who is the tallest?
questions.explanation: Chain: S > Q > P > R. S is tallest.
questions.trick: Convert every clue to a '>' sign and merge into one chain — S>Q>P>R falls out immediately.

questions.prompt: A mother is 3 times as old as her daughter. In 15 years, she will be twice as old as her daughter. Find the daughter's current age.
questions.trick: Add the future offset to BOTH ages before applying the new ratio: (3x+15) = 2×(x+15), never 3x = 2x+15.

questions.prompt: Pointing to a man, a woman says, 'He is the son of my father's only daughter.' If the woman has no siblings, how is the man related to her?
questions.explanation: 'My father's only daughter' = the woman herself (she has no siblings). So the man is her son.
questions.trick: Substitute 'my father's only daughter' with 'myself' immediately — the speaker has no siblings, so she IS that daughter.

questions.prompt: ₹7,000 is divided among A, B, C so that A gets twice as much as B, and B gets twice as much as C. Find C's share.
questions.explanation: Let C=x, B=2x, A=4x. Total=7x=7000 → x=1,000.

questions.prompt: Four friends sit in a row. B is immediately right of A. C is at the left end. D is not adjacent to A. Find the correct order.
questions.explanation: C at left end; A-B adjacent with B right of A; D not adjacent to A. Order C,A,B,D satisfies all three conditions.
questions.trick: Fix C at the end first (strongest clue), then slot the A-B adjacent pair, then check the negative D clue last.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q Q101): 'A factory always has ___.' Which of these must every factory have, by definition?
questions.explanation: A factory can exist without a chimney, without paid electricity, and without paper files — but not without workers/production activity, which defines what a factory is.
questions.trick: Eliminate any option you can imagine a real exception for; keep only what's true by definition.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q Q102): A book has 366 pages, numbered from page 1. How many total digits are used to number all the pages?
Using the same digit-counting method: how many total digits are used to number the pages of a 150-page book?

Real blood-relation puzzle (web-research): Among A, B, C, D, E — D (eldest) is a doctor, D's son is a lawyer, and the lawyer's wife is a teacher; B is male, E is not a professional's wife. How is D related to E?
questions.explanation: D, the eldest, pairs as husband to E — the doctor and his wife are the senior-most married pair in the tree.
questions.trick: Draw the family tree by generation first; resolve occupation clues into tree slots before answering.

questions.prompt: Same puzzle: who are the two females among A, B, C, D, E?
questions.explanation: B is stated male; D pairs with E as husband-wife; the lawyer (D's son) is male; A is the lawyer's wife (the teacher) — leaving E and A as the two females.
questions.trick: Reuse the tree drawn for the previous question rather than re-deriving from scratch.

questions.prompt: Six friends sit around a circular table, all facing the center. A is second to the right of B. D is directly opposite B. How is D placed relative to A?
options: Immediately to the right of A
Immediately to the left of A
Two seats to the right of A
questions.explanation: Fix B at position 0, seats numbered 0-5 clockwise. A = second to the right of B = position 2. D = opposite B (3 seats away on a 6-seat table) = position 3, which is exactly one step clockwise from A — i.e., immediately to A's right.
questions.trick: Fix one person's seat as position 0 before placing anyone else; convert every clue to a fixed seat number rather than visualizing the circle in your head.

questions.prompt: In a row of students, Meena is 9th from the left end and 15th from the right end. How many students are in the row?
The sum of a father's and son's ages is 50 years. Five years ago, the father's age was 4 times the son's age. Find the son's present age.
questions.explanation: Son=x, father=50−x. Five years ago: (50−x−5)=4(x−5) → 45−x=4x−20 → 65=5x → x=13. Son is 13 (father is 37; check: 5 years ago father was 32, son was 8, and 32=4×8 ✓).
questions.trick: Let son=x, father=(sum−x); apply the same past-year offset to both before using the ratio.

questions.prompt: ₹12,000 is divided among P, Q, R, S so that P gets twice Q, Q gets twice R, and R gets twice S. Find S's share.
questions.explanation: Ratio P:Q:R:S = 8:4:2:1 = 15 parts. 15 parts=12,000 → 1 part=800. S gets 1 part = ₹800.
questions.trick: Chain the ratio in one step: P:Q:R:S = 8:4:2:1, then divide total by the sum of parts.

questions.prompt: A woman says, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man she is pointing at?
questions.explanation: 'The only daughter of my mother' = the woman herself. So 'his mother' = the woman — she is his mother.
questions.trick: Substitute 'the only daughter of my mother' with 'myself' immediately, same technique as the father's-only-son pattern.
